results.possibleIdentity: Charles Scawthorn (also referred to as Charles R. Scawthorn or C. Scawthorn) is a prominent structural engineer and researcher specializing in the analysis and mitigation of natural and technological hazards, particularly earthquakes and fires. He attended The Cooper Union for the Advancement of Science and Art, where he earned a Bachelor of Engineering in 1966. He subsequently attended Lehigh University, receiving a Master of Science in Civil Engineering (M.S.C.E.) in 1968 with a thesis titled "Limit Analysis and Limit Equilibrium Solutions in Soil Mechanics." During his early career in the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was active as a structural engineer and later earned a doctorate (D.Eng.) from Kyoto University in 1981. Scawthorn has held several significant academic and professional roles, including Professor and Head of the Earthquake Disaster Prevention Systems Laboratory at Kyoto University (retired 2008), Visiting Professor at Stanford University and Waseda University, and Visiting Researcher at the University of California, Berkeley. He is the founder and President of SPA Risk LLC and co-founded EQECAT. He is widely recognized for developing the stochastic models for fires following earthquakes that became industry standards and for his leadership in developing the HAZUS-MH flood loss estimation methodology for FEMA. His professional background as a structural engineer with roots at Lehigh University matches historical records of technical persons associated with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which maintained a registry of volunteer engineers to collaborate with artists during that period.
